html:
When a Recover Makes Sense on Spartanburg's Industrial Roofs

A recover, installing a new roof system over an existing one rather than tearing off down to deck, is one of the most misunderstood options in commercial roofing. Done on the right roof, it saves real money and schedule time. Done on the wrong roof, it locks moisture underneath a new membrane and creates a bigger problem than the one it was meant to solve. We evaluate every recover candidate against a specific set of conditions before recommending it.

Recover Eligibility Under Code and Manufacturer Spec

Building code generally limits low-slope roofs to two existing membrane layers before a full tear-off is required, so the first question is how many roof systems are already on the deck. Beyond that, the existing insulation and deck have to be dry, which we confirm with core cuts and moisture scanning rather than a visual inspection alone. A roof that looks fine from the surface can still be holding moisture in the insulation layer that a recover would trap permanently.

Structural capacity matters too. Adding a new membrane and insulation layer adds dead load, and older buildings, including some of the converted mill structures around Spartanburg County, need that load checked against the original structural design before we commit to a recover over a tear-off. Where documentation on the original structure is thin, which is common on buildings that have changed hands or use several times, we recommend an engineer's evaluation rather than estimating capacity ourselves.

Correcting Drainage With Tapered Insulation

A recover is often the right moment to fix a drainage problem the existing roof never had solved. Installing tapered insulation as part of the recover package can redirect water toward drains that were previously undersized or poorly located, addressing ponding issues that a straight membrane replacement over the same substrate would simply repeat. We assess drainage as part of every recover evaluation, since correcting it during the project costs far less than fixing it as a separate job later.

Schedule and Operational Advantages

For manufacturing and distribution buildings tied into the automotive and tire supply chain around Greer, Duncan, and the broader I-85 corridor, a recover typically means less disruption to plant operations than a full tear-off, since there is no exposed deck period and no interior protection needed against sudden weather during construction. That scheduling advantage is a real factor for facilities running continuous production or fulfillment operations that cannot easily accommodate open-roof downtime.

Recover projects also generate less tear-off debris to haul and dispose of, which shows up as a real line-item savings in the bid compared to full replacement.

  • Layer count verification against code-allowed maximum
  • Moisture survey and core sampling of existing insulation and deck
  • Structural load check for the added recover assembly weight
  • Drainage evaluation and tapered insulation design where needed
  • New membrane system selection compatible with the existing substrate
  • Manufacturer warranty terms specific to recover versus new construction

Conditions That Disqualify an Overlay

We turn down recover jobs regularly when the moisture survey comes back wet, when the roof already carries two membrane layers, or when structural capacity does not support the additional dead load. In those cases, a recover is not a shortcut, it is a way to spend money now and spend more later fixing what the recover trapped. We tell owners that directly, with the moisture and structural findings documented, rather than quoting a recover because it is the easier sale.

Budget and Procurement Considerations

For facilities directors and procurement teams comparing capital options, a recover typically comes in below full tear-off and replacement cost while still qualifying for a meaningful manufacturer warranty when installed correctly. We provide both a recover and a full-replacement bid where the roof genuinely qualifies for either, so the decision reflects real numbers rather than a single option presented as the only path.

Documenting the Existing Roof Before We Start

Before any recover work begins, we document the existing roof condition with photos, moisture survey results, and a written summary of layer count and substrate findings. That record becomes part of the project file and gives the owner something concrete to reference if a warranty question comes up years later, rather than relying on memory of what was underneath the new membrane when it went down. It also gives the next contractor who works on the roof, whether that is us again or someone else entirely, an accurate starting point instead of guessing at what previous work is buried beneath the surface.

Common Questions About Recover Projects

How do you determine if my roof already has too many layers for a recover?

We confirm existing layer count during the site assessment, generally through core cuts, and compare it against the code-allowed maximum before quoting a recover.

What happens if moisture is found during the recover evaluation?

We recommend tear-off instead. Installing a recover over wet insulation traps that moisture and accelerates deck and insulation deterioration underneath the new roof.

Can a recover fix a roof that holds standing water?

In many cases, yes, by incorporating tapered insulation into the recover design to improve drainage toward existing or modified drain locations.

Is a recover always cheaper than full replacement?

Usually, but not always once tapered insulation or extensive flashing work is factored in. We quote both so the cost comparison is based on your specific roof.

Does a recover come with the same warranty as a new roof?

Warranty terms for recovers can differ from new construction warranties depending on the manufacturer and existing substrate. We document the specific terms in the bid.
